examples.title: Measuring Temperature
examples.content: You measure water temperature with a thermometer. It reads 25°C. But is it exactly 25.000...? Probably not. Thermometers have limits—they might only read to 0.5°C precision. Your measurement is 25°C ± 0.5°C. That's acknowledging uncertainty.

examples.title: Weighing an Object
examples.content: A scale says the book weighs 500g. But a better scale says 497g. Which is accurate? Maybe the true weight is 497.3g. Different tools have different precision. Knowing tool limits matters!

examples.title: Reaction Time Error
examples.content: You measure how long a ball takes to fall using a stopwatch. But your reaction time isn't perfect. You start/stop a bit late. This adds error. A good scientist would mention: 'Time = 2.1 seconds (±0.2 seconds reaction time).'
